what_it_does: Implements a deterministic, regex-based guardrail that inspects only the howto.what and howto.what_you_can_do fields of a dossier for structural code/design leakage and returns a pass/fail verdict with hit details.
observations: The module is framed as a 'voice floor' that makes the final leakage decision deterministically rather than relying on an LLM.
It checks exactly two fields the operator reads at a glance: howto.what and howto.what_you_can_do.
It uses a closed, mechanical deny-list of structural code shapes, not domain words.
Six compiled regex categories cover file paths/extensions, HTML/markup, CSS-like selectors, internal address schemes, feature-id shapes, and code identifiers.
Only the first match per pattern is recorded, truncated to 40 characters.
Domain vocabulary is explicitly allowed and never flagged.
standouts: Explicit anti-model-reliance design: 'the model raises, the floor decides'.
Very narrow, declared scope: two specific howto fields only.
Structural deny-list approach that deliberately excludes vocabulary-based blocking.
conventions: Uses a narrative module docstring to explain design rationale and scope.
Compiles regex patterns into a module-level tuple.
Uses simple type hints and returns a plain dict.
concerns: The file-path regex may match ordinary prose containing periods and slashes.
The identifier regex for camelCase requires at least two humps, so single-hump camelCase is not flagged.
The snake_case pattern only triggers when followed by '(', missing variable-like identifiers.
Only the first hit per category is reported, so repeated leakage in the same field is not enumerated.
notes: A narrow, deterministic regex floor for catching code-shaped leakage in two operator-facing prose fields, deliberately kept separate from model judgment.
questions: Why does the camelCase identifier regex require two humps rather than one?
Is the file-path regex intended to catch relative paths, or could it over-match normal punctuation?
How is the domain vocabulary allowlist maintained and verified?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose matches implementation: it deterministically checks two howto fields for structural code leakage using regex and returns pass/fail evidence.
intention_signals: Move the final verdict about code leakage out of the LLM and into a small, auditable, deterministic checker that focuses only on the prose an operator actually reads.
novelty: It inverts the usual LLM-as-judge pattern by using the model only to raise candidates while a regex floor decides, and it explicitly protects domain vocabulary from being flagged.
contribution: A deterministic, auditable prose-leakage filter scoped to two howto fields, returning structured evidence that a downstream fusion or review system can consume without re-running an LLM.
summary_for_embedding: prose_check.py implements a deterministic regex-based guardrail that checks only the howto.what and howto.what_you_can_do fields of a dossier for structural code leakage and returns a pass/fail result with hit details.
intention_for_embedding: Provide a model-independent floor for catching code and design substrate leakage in the two operator-facing prose snippets.

symbols.does: Inspects howto.what and howto.what_you_can_do for structural code leakage and returns a passed/hits dict.
symbols.description: Takes a dossier dict, extracts the two declared howto fields, concatenates them, searches the compiled leakage patterns, records the first match per category with a 40-character snippet, and returns a dict containing a boolean passed flag and a list of hit records.
